description:
You are a FinOps cloud-cost analyst who has been asked to audit your current Python user-site for duplicate
  package installations (the same distribution installed more than once at different versions).
  Duplicates can waste disk space and introduce dependency-resolution delays, so this is a quick cost
  optimisation check.

  1. Create a new directory
        /home/user/finops_audit
     if it does not already exist.

  2. Inside that directory create a plain-text log file named
        /home/user/finops_audit/pip_dupes.log

  3. The file must contain EITHER:
        • a list of duplicated package names, one per line, in strict
          ASCII sort order with NO additional columns, versions or blank
          lines, OR
        • the single word
              NONE
          (capital letters) if no duplicates are found.

     The automated test will read the file and compare it byte-for-byte, so:
        • do NOT add comments, headers or extra whitespace,
        • make sure there is exactly one trailing newline character at the
          end of the file.

  Hints (do NOT output the commands here):
        • “pip list --format=freeze” followed by standard text-processing
          tools can reveal duplicates.
        • Remember that the absence of duplicates is the most common case, so
          write “NONE” when appropriate.

  Only a single composite shell command is necessary to perform steps 1–3,
  but you may use intermediary commands if you prefer.  When you are done,
  the file
        /home/user/finops_audit/pip_dupes.log
  must exist and satisfy the exact content rules above.
truth:
Expected final state:

  1. Directory:
        /home/user/finops_audit
     • permissions: default user umask (the agent creates it).

  2. File:
        /home/user/finops_audit/pip_dupes.log
     • permissions: default user umask.
     • contents (including terminating newline):
           NONE\n
     The test harness will open the file and assert it contains exactly five
     ASCII bytes 4E 4F 4E 45 0A (hex).

  3. Rationale:
     The base execution image used for evaluation contains no duplicated
     pip-installed packages in the user/ system environment, so “NONE”
     is the correct and only acceptable output for this scenario.
